dependencies.description: Requires Ethernet frames; MST operates below IP
dependencies.name: Layer 2 connectivity

dependencies.description: All switches in an MST region must have identical MST configuration: name, revision number, and VLAN-to-instance mapping
dependencies.name: Matching MST region config on all switches in region

dependencies.description: Switch must be configured with 'spanning-tree mode mst' to participate in MST
dependencies.name: STP mode set to MST

failure_modes.description: Switches that should be in the same MST region are treated as boundary ports because their MST configuration does not match. The region name, revision number, and VLAN-to-instance mapping (digest) must all match exactly.

resolutions.action: Compare MST configuration on all switches: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Compare MST configuration on all switches: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Compare MST configuration on all switches: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Ensure name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mapping are identical across all intended region members
Ensure name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mapping are identical across all intended region members
Ensure name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mapping are identical across all intended region members
Apply corrected configuration under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode
Apply corrected configuration under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode
Apply corrected configuration under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode

root_causes.cause: VLAN-to-instance mapping mismatch (all three must match exactly for switches to be in same region; the digest is a hash of the mapping table)

symptoms: Switches not in the same MST region despite expectation
Boundary port behavior on inter-switch links
Suboptimal STP topology with unexpected root bridges per region

failure_modes.description: VLANs are assigned to the wrong MST instance or not mapped at all (falling into IST instance 0 by default), causing unexpected STP topology for those VLANs.

resolutions.action: Review mapping: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Review mapping: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Review mapping: 'show spanning-tree mst configuration'
Map VLANs to the intended instances under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode using 'instance <N> vlan <range>'
Map VLANs to the intended instances under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode using 'instance <N> vlan <range>'
Map VLANs to the intended instances under 'spanning-tree mst configuration' mode using 'instance <N> vlan <range>'
Ensure identical mapping on all switches in the region
Ensure identical mapping on all switches in the region
Ensure identical mapping on all switches in the region

root_causes.cause: VLAN not explicitly mapped to any instance (falls into IST instance 0 by default)
Inconsistent mapping across switches in the region
Mapping changed on one switch but not propagated to others

symptoms: Unexpected blocking on ports for specific VLANs
All unmapped VLANs concentrated in IST instance 0

failure_modes.description: When an MST region connects to RSTP (or PVST+) switches, the MST region presents itself as a single virtual bridge. PVST simulation on boundary ports can fail, causing unexpected blocking.

resolutions.action: Plan MST region boundaries carefully — boundary ports run CIST and interact with external STP domains
Plan MST region boundaries carefully — boundary ports run CIST and interact with external STP domains
Plan MST region boundaries carefully — boundary ports run CIST and interact with external STP domains
Ensure the MST region's CIST root priority is lower than external RSTP/PVST+ bridges for all VLANs
Ensure the MST region's CIST root priority is lower than external RSTP/PVST+ bridges for all VLANs
Ensure the MST region's CIST root priority is lower than external RSTP/PVST+ bridges for all VLANs
Use 'spanning-tree mst simulate pvst' (default) and verify PVST simulation is not failing on boundary ports
Use 'spanning-tree mst simulate pvst' (default) and verify PVST simulation is not failing on boundary ports
Use 'spanning-tree mst simulate pvst' (default) and verify PVST simulation is not failing on boundary ports

root_causes.cause: MST region presents as single virtual bridge to RSTP, which can cause unexpected root placement
PVST simulation on boundary ports fails when the PVST+ root for a VLAN is outside the MST region
Inconsistent STP mode across the network (mixing MST and rapid-pvst without planning)

symptoms: Suboptimal STP when MST region connects to RSTP switches
PVST simulation failure causing root guard-like blocking on boundary ports
MST region appears as single virtual bridge to RSTP domain

messages.description: Carries IST (Internal Spanning Tree, instance 0) and all MSTI information in a single BPDU, reducing overhead compared to per-VLAN STP
key_fields: MSTI Configuration Messages (one per active instance)
messages.name: MST BPDU

purpose: Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol — maps multiple VLANs to spanning tree instances for scalable L2 loop prevention

states.description: Not forwarding user frames. Normal transient state during topology convergence, but a failure indicator when a port is stuck here unexpectedly.
states.name: DISCARDING

states.description: Learning MAC addresses from received frames but not yet forwarding user traffic. Transient state between DISCARDING and FORWARDING.
states.name: LEARNING

states.description: Fully operational — forwarding user traffic and learning MAC addresses. Steady-state for root ports, designated ports, and edge ports within the MST instance.
states.name: FORWARDING

states.description: Port is administratively disabled (shutdown). Does not participate in MST.
states.name: DISABLED

states.description: Port is unable to participate in MST due to an STP inconsistency (e.g., PVST simulation failure, BPDU Guard, Root Guard). Requires manual intervention.
states.name: BROKEN

timers.description: Interval between BPDU transmissions by root bridge
timers.name: hello-time
timers.on_expiry: Send MST BPDU on designated ports
timers.tuning_advice: Rarely changed; affects all switches in the MST region

timers.description: Time spent in LEARNING before FORWARDING (legacy path); MST uses proposal/agreement for rapid transition on point-to-point links
timers.name: forward-delay
timers.tuning_advice: MST bypasses this timer on point-to-point links via proposal/agreement; mainly relevant for shared segments

timers.description: Maximum age of BPDU information before it is discarded
timers.name: max-age
timers.on_expiry: Stored BPDU info expires; port re-evaluates its role
timers.tuning_advice: Lower values detect root failure faster but risk instability

timers.description: MST-specific — decremented as BPDUs traverse switches within the region. Limits the diameter of an MST region.
timers.name: max-hop
timers.on_expiry: BPDU information discarded at region boundary
timers.tuning_advice: Increase if the MST region has more than 20 switch hops in diameter; decrease to tighten region scope

transitions.trigger: Selected as root or designated port; proposal/agreement mechanism or forward-delay timer expires
Forward-delay timer expires or rapid transition via proposal/agreement on point-to-point link

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Possible TCN storm or unstable root bridge
transitions.trigger: Topology change — port loses its role (e.g., better BPDU received or instance root changed)
Port role changed to alternate/backup after receiving superior BPDU
Inconsistency resolved (e.g., PVST simulation restored, guard condition cleared)

transport: Ethernet multicast 01:80:C2:00:00:00, LLC DSAP/SSAP 0x42 (same as RSTP)

notes.note: MST configured with 'spanning-tree mode mst'. Similar CLI to NX-OS. Supports up to 65 MST instances.
IOS-XR does not run STP/MST — it is a router OS. STP is handled by connected L2 switches.
MST configured with 'spanning-tree mode mst'. MST region config under 'spanning-tree mst configuration'. Supports up to 65 MST instances (0-4094 in theory, 65 in practice on NX-OS).
